title: Expressing support for the designation of January 30, 2025, as CTE (chronic traumatic encephalopathy) and RHI (repeated head impacts) Awareness Day.
plain_summary: This bill asks Congress to make January 30, 2025 a special day to teach people about a brain disease called CTE. CTE happens when someone gets hit in the head many times, like in sports or accidents. The disease can cause serious problems with thinking and behavior. Congress wants to thank the scientists who study this disease and help families who are dealing with it. They also want government health groups to teach everyone about what causes CTE and what the warning signs are.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Preamble
sections.text: This resolution was introduced in the House of Representatives on January twenty-eighth, twenty twenty-five by Representative Fitzpatrick and several other representatives. It was sent to the Committee on Energy and Commerce for review.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution expresses support for making January thirtieth, twenty twenty-five a special day to raise awareness about CTE (a brain disease called chronic traumatic encephalopathy) and RHI (repeated head impacts).

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: Chronic traumatic encephalopathy or CTE is a brain disease that gets worse over time. It happens in people who have had many head injuries over several years. In October twenty twenty-two, the National Institutes of Health officially agreed that getting hit in the head many times causes CTE. When people get hit in the head repeatedly, it damages the white matter in growing brains. Scientists have found that brains with CTE have a buildup of an abnormal protein called tau, which causes the brain to shrink. This tau protein is also found in Alzheimer's disease and other types of dementia. People with CTE may also show signs of other brain diseases like Alzheimer's, ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis), Lewy body disease, and Parkinson's disease. Athletes and military veterans have the highest risk of getting CTE because they often get hit in the head from contact sports or military training and explosions. However, CTE has also been found in people who survived domestic abuse and those with epilepsy. The symptoms of CTE and repeated head impacts include memory loss, shaking, addiction, worsening dementia, depression, thoughts of suicide, poor judgment, and paranoia. These symptoms usually appear years or decades after the brain injuries stop, but they have been found in a patient as young as seventeen years old. Right now, doctors can only confirm CTE after a person dies by examining their brain. Advocacy groups, doctors, and researchers are working hard to study what causes CTE and its symptoms. They want to help parents and families make good decisions about youth sports and create tests that can find CTE earlier so patients can get help sooner. Many foundations like the Patrick Risha CTE Awareness Foundation, the Chandler Kimball Foundation, the Mac Parkman Foundation, the Matthew Benedict's One Last Goal foundation, and the Zac Easter's CTE Hope foundation celebrate CTE and RHI Awareness Day across the United States. They use this day to remember those who died from CTE and repeated head impacts, help those suffering with the disease, and most importantly figure out how to prevent the disease.

sections.title: What the House of Representatives Supports
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports making CTE and RHI Awareness Day an official day of recognition. The House recognizes the doctors and researchers who study CTE and repeated head impacts, and thanks those who participate in research studies and donate their brains for research. The House recognizes people who may be suffering from CTE and repeated head impact symptoms, along with their families and caregivers. The House encourages the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and the National Institutes of Health to publish information about CTE and repeated head impacts in concussion education materials and teach the public about the symptoms and what might cause them. The House encourages all Americans to observe this day with appropriate ceremonies and activities that help people learn more about CTE and repeated head impacts.
